Fair Political Practices Commission 
Thomas Houston, Chairman 
1100 K Street 
.0. Box 807 
cramento, Cal. 95804 
Dear Mr. Houston: 
Enclosed is a copy of an oplnlon recently prepared 
el of Sacramento County for the Fair Oaks Community 
nning 
sory Council regarding the application of the Political Reform 
Act of 1974 to council proceedings. 
On behalf of the council, 
request that the commission or its legal staff review the opinion 
and inform me as to whether you agree with the conclusions rea 
d. 
The council is particularly interested in whether Section 
l8700(d)(2) of Title 2 of the California Administrative Code permits 
a council member to participate in the debate as a member of the 
public regarding a planning application for a project located 
adjacent to the council member's residence (see pages 5 and 6 of 
the op 
on). 
How broad is the exclusion made by Section l8700(d)(2) 
and what constitutes "personal interests" for purposes of the regulation? 
A copy of the letter requesting the county counsel oplnlon is 
also enclosed for your information. 
nk you for your assistance 
i 
matter. 
Sincerely, 
Michael J. Kersten 
Chairperson, 
Fair Oaks Community Planning 
Advisory Council

Toby Johnson, Sacramento County Board of Supervisors 
All council members 


COUNTY OF SACRAMENTO 
THE COUNTY COUNSEL 
l. 8. ElAM 
County Counsel 
FRED G. WilLIAMS 
Assistant County Counsel 
THOMAS A. DARliNG 
ROBERT S. WILLETT 
ROBERT L. PLE;NES 
Supervising Deputy 
County Counsels 
700 H STREET, SUITE 2650 
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 911814 
PHONE (g 16) 440'5544 
December 4, 1980 
Michael J. Kersten, Chairperson 
Fair Oaks Community Planning Advisory Council 
8224 Hegseth Court 
Fair Oaks, CA 95628 
DEPUTY CDUNTY COUNSELS 
ALFRED.I. GUETLING 
CLEMENT.I. DOUGHERTY • .JR. 
RYAN M. POLSTRA 
MONTE L. FULLER 
RICHARD D. MAYER 
WILLIAM ~I. FOWLER 
.lOSE PH M. TAILLEFER 
J. STEVEN BURRIS 
LAWRE:NCE A. JONES 
ELAINE P. DIPIETRO 
MELVYN W. PRICE 
MANUEL E. LOPES 
LILLY C. FRAWL.EY 
FRANK M. GARCIA 
MARGARET L. HAGERTY 
H. DESMOND MCINTOSH 
JOEL. Y.. UHER 
RE: 
Conflict of Interest - Political Reform Act 1974 
Government Code Section 87100 
Dear Mr. Kersten: 
We have your letter dated October 1, 1980, which requests we 
issue written opinions on the following questions: 
1. 
Must a community council member disqualify himself 
or herself when reviewing a planning application involving 
property located in the same neighborhood as the residence 
of a council member~ 
You indicate that normally this 
involves property located within 500 feet of the member's 
residence" 
2. 
Must a council member remain silent during 
deliberations of the council, if the council member has 
a financial interest in a planning application which is 
being reviewed by the council? 
3. 
If the council member who has a financial 
interest in the planning application is the chairperson 
of the council, must that person relinquish the chair 
during the council's deliberation on the planning appli-
cation? 
4. 
If a council member has a financial interest in 
a planning application, may that council member disclOSe 
the financial interest, disqualify himself from partici 
tion in the deliberation as a council person, but participate 


Michael J. Kersten 
-2-
December 4, 1980 
in the debate and address the council as a member of 
the public? 
Further, must that council person resign his 
or her membership in the council to participate in the 
debate as a member of the public? 
We are not informed as to the nature of the financial 
interest by the letter of October 1, so for the purposes of this 
opinion, we assume that the financial interest is a material 
financial interest as set forth in County regulations which are 
attached as Exhibit "A" to this opinion letter. l 
OPINION 
In response to question No.1, it is our opInIon that a coun-
cil member should disqualify himself or herself when his or her 
position is so biased so as to substantially color his or her 
opinion with regard to the application pending before the coun-
cil. 
The proponent for the application is entitled to a fair and 
impartial hearing which may not be possible if a council person 
has a bias with regard to the pending application. 
In response to questions No. 2 and 3, it is our opinion that 
a council person who has a material financial interest in a 
matter pending before the council should disclose the financial 
interest, disqualify himself or herself from voting on the matter 
and refrain from participating in the deliberations of the coun-
cil. 
If this involves the chairperson or vice-chairperson, the 
chair should be assumed by another council person pursuant to 
council rules. 
In response to question No.4, it is our opinion that the 
council person who has disclosed a material financial interest 
should not participate in any debate on the matter pending before 
the council, except perhaps, in the limited circumstance when he 
or she is the owner of, or has a substantial financial interest in 
the property which is the subject of the debate. 
ANALYSIS 
The questions asked involve two completely different concepts 
of law. 
The first relates to the motive or bias of a trier of 
fact and the constitutional demand that a person is entitled to 
a fair and impartial hearing. 
The other questions deal with 
the broad field commonly referred to a "conflict of interest" 
1 
The County's regulations attached as Exhibit "A" defining 
material interests are based upon Government Code Section 87103 
and sets forth the nature of the financial interest described in 
the Political Reform Act of 1974. 


Michael J. Kersten 
-3-
December 4, 1980 
and generally involve an opportunity for personal or pecuniary 
gain or aggrandizement. 
Your first question deals with whether a proponent will be 
afforded a fair hearing. 
Although a claim of bias or improper 
motive can be claimed, to successfully prove a due process viola-
tion due to bias or improper motivation, there must be a suf-
ficient showing that a property owner's rights were jeopardized 
by the bias. 
Case law on the subject has evolved from hearing 
decision makers rather than advisory groups but we believe the 
rationale of the cases is pertinent particularly when there is 
substantial reliance on advisory opinions as in the case of the 
opinions given to decision makers by your council. 
In Sladovich 
vs. Coun~of Fresno (1958) 158 Cal. App. 2d 230, 241, the plain-
tiff claimed bias on the part of a planning commissioner because 
the commissioner erroneously believed that a particular zoning 
classification on plaintiff's property would detrimentally affect 
the commissioner's property and that he failed to disclose the 
interest to the other commissioners. 
The Court held, however, 
that even if such bias existed, it would not necessarily void the 
action to remaining members, since all other members voted to 
deny the petition and there was no sufficient showing that 
plaintiff's rights were jeopardized. 
It is a general rule that 
inquiry into motive appears to be equally applicable to admi-
nistrative as well as legislative decisions relating to zoning 
matters. 
It has been stated by a court and ruling on a legisla-
tive determination that the "purpose or motive of the city offi-
cials in passing an ordinance is irrelevant to any inquiry 
concerning the reasonableness of the ordinance .. 
if the con-
ditions justify the enactment of the ordinance, the motives 
prompting its enactment are of no consequence. 
If the conditions 
do not justify the enactment! the inquiry as to motive becomes 
useless 
"McCarthy vs. City of Manhattan Beach (19S3) 41 
Cal. 2d 879, 894. 
See also Ensign Bickford Realty Corp. vs. City 
Council (1977) 68 Cal. App. 3d 467, 478 and Schroeder vs. 
Municipal Court (1977) 73 Cal. App. 3d 841, 847. 
The general rule also applies to quasi-judicial zoning 
determinations. 
In City of Fairfield vs. Superior Court (1975) 
14 Cal. 3d 768, the Supreme Court upheld a city council's admi-
nistrative denial of an application for a planned unit develop-
ment to permit construction of a shopping center. 
In so doing, 
the Supreme Court ruled that inquiry into the reasoning process 
of a council person relative to their rejection of the permit was 
improper. 
Second, the Court held that inquiries seeking to 
discover evidence to support a charge that the council person was 
biased was also irrelevant. 
Each council person who has property proximate to a proposed 
development must judge whether he or she is biased with regard to 
the project. 
The question of any bias, of course, can be (and 


Michael J. Kersten 
-4-
December 4, 1980 
has in the past) been raised by developers at Planning Commission 
and/or Board of Supervisors level when the project passes through 
the approval chain. 
It would be our recommendatin that if any 
question of bias exists, the council person should disqualify 
himself and announce the reason therefor. 
An exception to this 
perhaps would be the situation where the vote of a council person 
is necessary in order to take a position by the council on the 
particular application. 
Questions 2, 3, and 4 relate to the doctrine of "conflict of 
interest". 
Under California law; there are four types of 
conflicts which can disqualify an individual. 
These are generally 
as follows: 
1. 
A person may not hold two public offices at the 
same time, if those offices are incompatible; 
2. 
The Political Reform Act of 1974 (Government 
Code Section 87100 et seq.) prohibits public officials from 
participating in decisions in which they possess an economic 
interest; 
3. 
Government Code, Section 1090 et seq., prohibits 
public officers or employees from participating in 
decisions involving contracts in which they are financially 
interested; and 
4. 
Government Code Section 1126(a) prohibits 
public officers and employees from engaging in incompatible 
employment or activities for compensation. 
We believe your activities are controlled by the Political 
Reform Act2 
(No. 2 above) and we limit our analysis accordingly. 
The Act precludes certain public officials from participating 
in decision making when the official has a financial interest in 
the pending matter. 
Section 87100 of the Government Code (part 
of the Act) reads: 
2 
"No public official at any level of state or 
or local government shall make, participate in 
making or in any way attempt to use his official 
position to influence a governmental decision in 
which he knows or has reason to know he has a 
financial interest." 
Hereinafter "the Act". 


Michael J. Kersten 
-5-
December 4, 1980 
Section 87200 defines the applicability of the Act. 
"This article is applicable to elected 
officers, judges of courts of record, meillbers 
of the Public utilities Commission, members of 
the State Energy Resources Conservation and 
Development Commission, members of the Fair 
Political Practices Commission, members of the 
board of supervisors, district attorneys and 
chief administrative officers of counties, 
mayors, city managers, chief administrative 
officers and members of city councils of cities, 
members of each of the six regional coastal 
commissions, and to candidates for any of these 
offices at any election." 
It is clear from the above that advisory councils are not 
included within the scope of the Act per see 
However, the County 
of Sacramento has gone beyond the scope of the Act and has speci-
fically included Planning Advisory Councils within the County's 
implementation of the Act. 
Section 2.36.080 of the Sacramento 
County Code requires appointees to advisory councils to comply 
with all State and County laws, ordinances and regulations 
relating to conflicts of interest and financial disclosures. 
The 
County's regulations (Chapter 2.65 of the County Code and regula-
tions adopted thereunder), implementing the Act coupled with the 
requirements of Section 2.36.080 make it quite clear that the 
Board of Supervisors intended to include all municipal advisory 
councils within the conflict of interest regulations. 
In view of 
the reliance of the Board of Supervisors and Planning 
Commissioners on council advice, we think this inclusive is 
justified. 
As stated, supra, if a council member has a material 
financial interest in an application he or she must not par-
ticipate in the council's deliberations. 
To do so would conflict 
with the County's implementation of the Act. 
We further believe 
the council person must step down from the chair if the conflict 
exists. 
Your letter further inquires as to whether the person with 
the conflict could participate in the debate before the council 
as a member of the public. 
The Attorney General recently 
answered a similar question in regard to a disqualified LAFCO 
member who desired to participate as a citizen in a debate or a 
proposed annexation to a city. 
The Attorney General found the 
LAFCO member to be disqualified but excepted the member from the 
disqualification if the member owned property within the terri-
tory proposed to be annexed. 
The Attorney General based his opi-
nion on regulations promUlgated by the Fair Political Practices 
Commission (FPPC) implementing the Act (See 610 Ops. Atty. Gen. 
396) . 


Michael J. Kersten 
-6-
Dece~ber 4, 1980 
Based upon the Attorney General's position and the regula-
tions of the FPPC, it is our opinion that a member of your council 
who has disqualified himself and announced the basis thereof 
may participate in the debate before the council if he has a 
substantial interest in or is the owner of property which is the 
subject of the council's deliberations. 
Except in this limited 
situation, we are of the opinion the council person should not 
participate in the debate. 
We trust this answers the questions you have posed, if there 
are additional questions which relate to this opinion, please 
feel free to contact the undersigned. 
RSW:ph 
cc: 
Tobias Johnson, 
Supervisor, Fifth District 
Respectfully submitted, 
L. B. ELAM, 
County Counsel 
By 
I' 
i 
.. / 
/C 
~C j L. ·c, .<.~L . ./ 
R. S. WILLETT 
Supervising Deputy 
All Council Members, Fair Oaks 
Community Planning Advisory Council 


Lee Elam 
Sacramento County Counsel 
700 H Street 
Sacramento, Calif. 
95814 
Dear Mr. Elam: 
October 1, 1980 
On behalf of the Fair Oaks COffil.llunity Planning Advisory Council, 
I would like to request your written legal opinion on the extent to 
which a council member must disqualify himself when reviewing a 
planning application for property located in the same neighborhood 
as the council member's residence. 
The most typical fact situation 
involves a proposed development of property located within 500 feet 
of the member's residence. 
Also, assuming the member has a financial interest in the 
decision of whether to approve a particular application, must the 
member remain silent during all council deliberations on the 
application? 
In such case, may the member disclose his financial 
interest, join the public audience, and address the council regarding 
the application solely in his capacity as a neighborhood resident 
and private citizen, or must the member resign in order to make 
any statement regarding the application? 
If the finan~ially interested 
member is the council chairperson, must he relinquish the chair 
for the duration of the deliberations on the application? 
In order to facilitate council action on pending planning 
applications, the council would appreciate your rasponse by 
November 1, 1980. 
Thank you, 
Sincerely, 
Michael J. Kersten 
Chairperson, 
Fair Oaks Co~~unity Planning 
Advisory Council 
cc: 
Toby Johnson, Sacramento County Board of Supervisors 
All council members

of bias exists, the council person should disqualify 
himself and announce the reason therefor. 
An exception to this 
perhaps would be the situation where the vote of a council person 
is necessary in order to take a position by the council on the 
particular application.

interest".

financial interest."

Michael J. Kersten 
December 4, 1980

officers, judges of courts of record, meillbers 
of the Public utilities Commission, members of 
the State Energy Resources Conservation and 
Development Commission, members of the Fair

chief administrative officers of counties,

commissions, and to candidates for any of these

It is clear from the above that advisory councils are not

County Code requires appointees to advisory councils to comply

councils within the conflict of interest regulations. 
In view of 
the reliance of the Board of Supervisors and Planning 
Commissioners on council advice, we think this inclusive is 
justified. 
As stated, supra, if a council member has a material 
financial interest in an application he or she must not par-
ticipate in the council's deliberations. 
To do so would conflict 
with the County's implementation of the Act. 
We further believe 
the council person must step down from the chair if the conflict 
exists. 
Your letter further inquires as to whether the person with 
the conflict could participate in the debate before the council 
as a member of the public. 
The Attorney General recently 
answered a similar question in regard to a disqualified LAFCO 
member who desired to participate as a citizen in a debate or a 
proposed annexation to a city. 
The Attorney General found the 
LAFCO member to be disqualified but excepted the member from the 
disqualification if the member owned property within the terri-
tory proposed to be annexed. 
The Attorney General based his opi-
nion on regulations promUlgated by the Fair Political Practices 
Commission (FPPC) implementing the Act (See 610 Ops. Atty. Gen. 
396) . 


Michael J. Kersten 
Dece~ber 4, 1980 
Based upon the Attorney General's position and the regula-
tions of the FPPC, it is our opinion that a member of your council 
who has disqualified himself and announced the basis thereof 
may participate in the debate before the council if he has a 
substantial interest in or is the owner of property which is the 
subject of the council's deliberations. 
Except in this limited 
situation, we are of the opinion the council person should not 
participate in the debate. 
We trust this answers the questions you have posed, if there 
are additional questions which relate to this opinion, please 
feel free to contact the undersigned. 
RSW:ph 
cc: 
Tobias Johnson, 
Supervisor, Fifth District
sections.conclusion: A council member should disqualify himself or herself when his or her position is so biased so as to substantially color his or her opinion with regard to the application pending before the council. A council person who has a material financial interest in a matter pending before the council should disclose the financial interest, disqualify himself or herself from voting on the matter and refrain from participating in the deliberations of the council. A disqualified council member may appear before the council as a member of the public on matters related solely to his or her personal financial interest, but must refrain from participating in deliberations as a council member.

The Court held, however, 
that even if such bias existed, it would not necessarily void the 
action to remaining members, since all other members voted to 
deny the petition and there was no sufficient showing that 
plaintiff's rights were jeopardized. 
It is a general rule that 
inquiry into motive appears to be equally applicable to admi-
nistrative as well as legislative decisions relating to zoning 
matters. 
It has been stated by a court and ruling on a legisla-
tive determination that the "purpose or motive of the city offi-
cials in passing an ordinance is irrelevant to any inquiry 
concerning the reasonableness of the ordinance .. 
if the con-
ditions justify the enactment of the ordinance, the motives 
prompting its enactment are of no consequence. 
If the conditions 
do not justify the enactment! the inquiry as to motive becomes 
useless 
"McCarthy vs. City of Manhattan Beach (19S3) 41 
Cal. 2d 879, 894. 
See also Ensign Bickford Realty Corp. vs. City 
Council (1977) 68 Cal. App. 3d 467, 478 and Schroeder vs. 
Municipal Court (1977) 73 Cal. App. 3d 841, 847. 
The general rule also applies to quasi-judicial zoning 
determinations. 
In City of Fairfield vs. Superior Court (1975) 
14 Cal. 3d 768, the Supreme Court upheld a city council's admi-
nistrative denial of an application for a planned unit develop-
ment to permit construction of a shopping center. 
In so doing, 
the Supreme Court ruled that inquiry into the reasoning process 
of a council person relative to their rejection of the permit was 
improper. 
Second, the Court held that inquiries seeking to 
discover evidence to support a charge that the council person was 
biased was also irrelevant. 
Each council person who has property proximate to a proposed 
development must judge whether he or she is biased with regard to 
the project. 
The question of any bias, of course, can be (and 


Michael J. Kersten 
December 4, 1980 
has in the past) been raised by developers at Planning Commission 
and/or Board of Supervisors level when the project passes through 
the approval chain. 
It would be our recommendatin that if any
sections.question_synthetic: The Fair Oaks Community Planning Advisory Council seeks guidance on conflict of interest and disqualification requirements under the Political Reform Act of 1974, specifically whether council members must disqualify themselves from planning applications affecting nearby properties, whether they must remain silent during deliberations if financially interested, and whether they may participate as members of the public if they disclose their interest.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Council members with material financial interests must disqualify themselves from voting and deliberating on the matter. However, a disqualified member may participate as a member of the public to address matters related solely to their personal financial interest, such as their residence or business, without resigning from the council. The scope of participation is limited to appearing and representing personal interests in the same manner as any other member of the public.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr; LLM: Document is OCR-scanned with significant degradation and scanning artifacts affecting readability throughout. Contains multiple embedded documents: initial request from council chairperson (January 22, 1981), county counsel opinion (December 4, 1980), and FPPC response letter (March 4, 1981). FPPC response by Diane Maura Fishburn clarifies and elaborates on county counsel's opinion regarding Government Code Sections 87100, 87103, and administrative code sections 18700, 18702. High OCR error rate reduces confidence in exact verbatim text extraction, but core substantive content is discernible.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: of bias exists, the council person should disqualify 
himself and announce the reason therefor. 
An exception to this 
perhaps would be the situation where the vote of a council person 
is necessary in order to take a position by the council on the 
particular application. 
Questions 2, 3, and 4 relate to the doctrine of "conflict of 
interest". 
Under California law; there are four types of 
conflicts which can disqualify an individual. 
These are generally 
as follows: 
1. 
A person may not hold two public offices at the 
same time, if those offices are incompatible; 
2. 
The Political Reform Act of 1974 (Government 
Code Section 87100 et seq.) prohibits public officials from 
participating in decisions in which they possess an economic 
interest; 
3. 
Government Code, Section 1090 et seq., prohibits 
public officers or employees from participating in 
decisions involving contracts in which they are financially 
interested; and 
4. 
Government Code Section 1126(a) prohibits 
public officers and employees from engaging in incompatible 
employment or activities for compensation. 
We believe your activities are controlled by the Political 
Reform Act2 
(No. 2 above) and we limit our analysis accordingly. 
The Act precludes certain public officials from participating 
in decision making when the official has a financial interest in 
the pending matter. 
Section 87100 of the Government Code (part 
of the Act) reads: 
2 
"No public official at any level of state or 
or local government shall make, participate in 
making or in any way attempt to use his official 
position to influence a governmental decision in 
which he knows or has reason to know he has a 
financial interest." 
Hereinafter "the Act". 


Michael J. Kersten 
December 4, 1980 
Section 87200 defines the applicability of the Act. 
"This article is applicable to elected 
officers, judges of courts of record, meillbers 
of the Public utilities Commission, members of 
the State Energy Resources Conservation and 
Development Commission, members of the Fair 
Political Practices Commission, members of the 
board of supervisors, district attorneys and 
chief administrative officers of counties, 
mayors, city managers, chief administrative 
officers and members of city councils of cities, 
members of each of the six regional coastal 
commissions, and to candidates for any of these 
offices at any election." 
It is clear from the above that advisory councils are not 
included within the scope of the Act per see 
However, the County 
of Sacramento has gone beyond the scope of the Act and has speci-
fically included Planning Advisory Councils within the County's 
implementation of the Act. 
Section 2.36.080 of the Sacramento 
County Code requires appointees to advisory councils to comply 
with all State and County laws, ordinances and regulations 
relating to conflicts of interest and financial disclosures. 
The 
County's regulations (Chapter 2.65 of the County Code and regula-
tions adopted thereunder), implementing the Act coupled with the 
requirements of Section 2.36.080 make it quite clear that the 
Board of Supervisors intended to include all municipal advisory 
councils within the conflict of interest regulations. 
In view of 
the reliance of the Board of Supervisors and Planning 
Commissioners on council advice, we think this inclusive is 
justified. 
As stated, supra, if a council member has a material 
financial interest in an application he or she must not par-
ticipate in the council's deliberations. 
To do so would conflict 
with the County's implementation of the Act. 
We further believe 
the council person must step down from the chair if the conflict 
exists. 
Your letter further inquires as to whether the person with 
the conflict could participate in the debate before the council 
as a member of the public. 
The Attorney General recently 
answered a similar question in regard to a disqualified LAFCO 
member who desired to participate as a citizen in a debate or a 
proposed annexation to a city. 
The Attorney General found the 
LAFCO member to be disqualified but excepted the member from the 
disqualification if the member owned property within the terri-
tory proposed to be annexed. 
The Attorney General based his opi-
nion on regulations promUlgated by the Fair Political Practices 
Commission (FPPC) implementing the Act (See 610 Ops. Atty. Gen. 
396) . 


Michael J. Kersten 
Dece~ber 4, 1980 
Based upon the Attorney General's position and the regula-
tions of the FPPC, it is our opinion that a member of your council 
who has disqualified himself and announced the basis thereof 
may participate in the debate before the council if he has a 
substantial interest in or is the owner of property which is the 
subject of the council's deliberations. 
Except in this limited 
situation, we are of the opinion the council person should not 
participate in the debate. 
We trust this answers the questions you have posed, if there 
are additional questions which relate to this opinion, please 
feel free to contact the undersigned. 
RSW:ph 
cc: 
Tobias Johnson, 
Supervisor, Fifth District

CONCLUSION: A council member should disqualify himself or herself when his or her position is so biased so as to substantially color his or her opinion with regard to the application pending before the council. A council person who has a material financial interest in a matter pending before the council should disclose the financial interest, disqualify himself or herself from voting on the matter and refrain from participating in the deliberations of the council. A disqualified council member may appear before the council as a member of the public on matters related solely to his or her personal financial interest, but must refrain from participating in deliberations as a council member.

embedding.summary: This FPPC advice letter addresses conflict of interest requirements for a planning advisory council under the Political Reform Act of 1974, providing guidance on disqualification procedures, disclosure requirements, and the limited circumstances under which a disqualified council member may participate as a member of the public.
